messages.content: Build from scratch: given a list of user IDs, look up each via a TaskEither-returning lookup function and collect all users with A.traverse(TE.ApplicativePar). Fail fast on first error.
Build from scratch: validate a list of raw strings as positive integers using A.traverse with Either. Return Either<string, ReadonlyArray<number>>.
How do I turn ReadonlyArray<Option<A>> into Option<ReadonlyArray<A>> using A.sequence? Show a short example.
Explain briefly how A.traverse with TaskEither differs between sequential and parallel applicative, then show a working typed implementation.

Refactor this naive loop into A.traverse with Either so errors propagate as E.left and success yields a full array: const parse = (xs: string[]) => { const out: number[] = []; for (const x of xs) { const n = Number(x); if (Number.isNaN(n)) return null; out.push(n); } return out; }

Constraint-driven: write a function that reads a ReadonlyArray<string> of config paths, loads each (returning TaskEither<LoadError, string>), and returns all contents concatenated. Constraints: use A.traverse parallel, tagged LoadError, no any.
Build from scratch: over a Record<string, number> of raw amounts, use R.map to convert all values to cents (x * 100). Return the record typed as Record<string, number>.
How do I filter a Record<string, number> to keep only positive values and simultaneously transform them with R.filterMap? Show a typed example.
Build from scratch: traverse a Record<string, string> of raw form fields with R.traverse(E.Applicative) where each field parses into a number. Return E.Either<string, Record<string, number>>.

Explain-then-implement: explain briefly how R.traverse carries applicative effects across record values, then show a typed implementation that resolves a Record<string, string> of user IDs into a Record<string, User> via a TaskEither lookup, parallel.

Targeted fix: convert this imperative record mapping into R.map with fp-ts: function rename(r: Record<string, { name: string }>) { const out: Record<string, string> = {}; for (const k in r) out[k] = r[k].name; return out; }

Capability add-on: starting from this R.map pipeline that doubles values, also drop keys whose doubled value exceeds 100: export const doubled = (r: Record<string, number>) => R.map((v: number) => v * 2)(r);
Build from scratch: a State<number, number> computation named pushPop that increments a counter by 1 on each run and returns the previous counter value. Use fp-ts State.
How do I compose three State<S, A> steps so state threads through and each step sees the result of the prior? Show a minimal example with S = string[].
Build from scratch: a pure stack interpreter using State<ReadonlyArray<number>, void>. Provide push, pop (returns Option<number>), and a program that pushes 1, 2, 3 and then pops once.

Constraint-driven: write a State-monad pipeline that renders a simple expression AST ({kind:'lit', n:number} | {kind:'add', l:AST, r:AST}) into a linear bytecode while threading a counter for label IDs. Constraints: no mutation outside State, no any, all fields readonly.

Explain-then-implement: explain briefly how a Reader<R, A> encodes dependency injection, then implement a pricing function that reads a TaxConfig from the environment and computes a total for a list of line items.
Build from scratch: a Reader<Env, string> program with Env = { greeting: string, name: string } that combines two Reader values with RE.ap to build a full sentence.
How do I use Reader.local to temporarily modify the environment for a sub-computation? Show a typed example.
Build from scratch: a Reader-based DI setup for a service that looks up users. Define UserRepo interface, a findById Reader<{repo: UserRepo}, Option<User>>, and show wiring with a stub repo.
Constraint-driven: build a Reader-based formatter for monetary amounts. Env has locale and currency. Provide formatOne and formatMany. Constraints: no mutation, reuse Intl.NumberFormat, no any.

Refactor this string concatenation into fp-ts/Monoid form so the separator is handled via the string monoid and fold: function csv(xs: string[]) { let out = ''; for (let i = 0; i < xs.length; i++) out += (i === 0 ? '' : ',') + xs[i]; return out; }
How do I build a Monoid for a custom record like { total: number, lines: ReadonlyArray<string> }? Show a minimal example using M.struct.
Build from scratch: a Semigroup<ReadonlyArray<number>> that keeps only unique sorted values when concatenating. Provide concat and fold across an array of arrays.
How do I combine multiple validator result arrays using the array semigroup so all errors accumulate? Show with string errors.
Constraint-driven: define a Monoid<Stats> where Stats has count, sum, and max fields. Constraints: max uses Math.max semantics with -Infinity as empty, all fields readonly, use M.struct.
Capability add-on: extend this Monoid so it also tracks min: export const StatsM = M.struct({ count: N.MonoidSum, sum: N.MonoidSum });
Build from scratch: given Option<Either<string, number>>, write a function that maps the inner number to its square while preserving both outer layers. No .pipe on the functors.
How do I flip Either<E, Option<A>> into Option<Either<E, A>> while preserving types? Show a minimal example.

Targeted fix: this loop mixes Option handling and imperative summing; rewrite using O.chain and A.filterMap so None values are dropped: const sumOfSquares = (xs: (number | null)[]) => { let t = 0; for (const x of xs) if (x !== null) t += x*x; return t; }
Explain-then-implement: explain briefly how Option<Either<E, A>> composes as two stacked functors, then show a helper that maps a function over the innermost A.
Build from scratch: TaskEither<Error, Option<User>> describing a lookup that may resolve to none. Chain it so a missing user becomes a tagged NotFound error on the left side.

Constraint-driven: write a function that produces a Task<number> returning 42 after 10 ms and then runs two copies back-to-back. Constraints: use A.sequence with T.ApplicativeSeq, readonly result, no Promise.all, no any.
How do I run an array of Task values in parallel and gather results? Show a minimal example with T.ApplicativePar.
Explain-then-implement: explain briefly how Task differs from Promise and when you should pick Task, then show a chained Task<string> example with T.chain.
Explain briefly how T.delay interacts with T.ap in fp-ts Task, then show a typed example where a delayed task and an instant task are applied together.

Constraint-driven: write a Task pipeline that fetches three independent pieces of data in parallel and combines them into a dashboard record. Constraints: no Promise.all, only fp-ts Task, readonly fields, no any.
Capability add-on: take this Task pipeline and add a 100 ms delay between the two fetches so they are sequential with a pause: const prog = pipe(fetchA, T.chain(() => fetchB));

Capability add-on: given this parallel traverse, add a summary stage that counts the successes and rebuilds as a Record keyed by id: const fetchAll = (ids: string[]) => A.traverse(TE.ApplicativePar)(lookup)(ids);

Build from scratch: a validator that takes a ReadonlyArray<{ age: number }> and returns Either<ReadonlyNonEmptyArray<string>, ReadonlyArray<number>> using validation accumulation. Every age must be >= 0 and finite; collect all failures.
